title: OpenPrescribing in R
text:
Overview
This tutorial walks you through how to work with the OpenPrescribing.net BigQuery data in R. It will cover:

Connect to BigQuery from R using DBI1 and bigrquery2
Description of the main tables in the BigQuery database** dm3
Reusable functions in R using dbplyr4
Applied example following PICK AN EXAMPLE STUDY WE DID





1. R Special Interest Group on Databases (R-SIG-DB), Wickham, H. & Müller, K. DBI: R Database Interface. (2024). doi:10.32614/CRAN.package.DBI.


2. Wickham, H. & Bryan, J. bigrquery: An Interface to Google’s ’BigQuery’ ’API’. (2025). doi:10.32614/CRAN.package.bigrquery.


3. Schieferdecker, T., Müller, K. & Bergant, D. dm: Relational Data Models. (2025). doi:10.32614/CRAN.package.dm.


4. Wickham, H., Girlich, M. & Ruiz, E. dbplyr: A ’Dplyr’ Back End for Databases. (2024). doi:10.32614/CRAN.package.dbplyr.

title: 1  Connect to BigQuery
text:
1.1 Get access
To get access to the OpenPrescribing BigQuery database write a message in the #openprescribing Slack channel and tag slack-admin. One of the admins will send you a JSON file with your BigQuery credentials in a private message. The file should look something like this:
IMPORTANT: Never commit this file to GitHub or share it with anyone.

title: 1  Connect to BigQuery
text:
1.2 Store your credentials
You can store this file anywhere on your computer, but a common location is in a hidden folder in your home directory, for example:
On macOS/Linux:
~/.config/openprescribing/bq-credentials.json
On Windows:
C:/Users/YOUR_USERNAME/.config/openprescribing/bq-credentials.json

title: 1  Connect to BigQuery
text:
1.3 Set up env variable
Rather than hardcoding the path in your R scripts you can set an environment variable named OP_CREDENTIALS or similar. To help you edit your .Renviron file and set the variable for all R sessions you can use the usethis package:

# Open your .Renviron file
usethis::edit_r_environ()

This will open your .Renviron file in your editor where you can add the location where you stored your credentials file, for example:
# macOS/Linux
OP_CREDENTIALS=~/.config/openprescribing/bq-credentials.json

# Windows
OP_CREDENTIALS=C:/Users/YOUR_USERNAME/.config/openprescribing/bq-credentials.json
Note: You will probably need to restart your R session for R to know about your new environment variable.

title: 1  Connect to BigQuery
text:
1.4 Connet to BigQuery
The following code connects to the hscic dataset in the ebmdatalab project. You will need to authenticate using the path to your credentials file stored in the OP_CREDENTIALS environment variable and might be asked to login with your Google account the first time you run this.

library(DBI)
library(bigrquery)

bq_auth(path = Sys.getenv("OP_CREDENTIALS"))

con <- dbConnect(
  bigrquery::bigquery(),
  project = "ebmdatalab",
  dataset = "hscic",
  billing = "ebmdatalab"
)

title: 2  Database schema
text:
2.1 Main hscic tables
The hscic dataset has 194 different tables. Here we describe the main tables used for research projects. To see all the tables available in the hscic dataset, you can use:
dbListTables(con)

title: 2  Database schema
text:
2.1.1 normalised_prescribing
Normalised view of the main prescribing dataset, updated monthly. This is the table you’ll typically want to query rather than the raw prescribing table.








Columns






sha
character
?


regional_team
character
Regional team identifier


stp
character
Sustainability and Transformation Partnership code


pct
character
Primary Care Trust code


practice
character
Practice code


bnf_code
character
British National Formulary (BNF) code


bnf_name
character
British National Formulary (BNF) drug name


items
integer
Number of items (one item = one appearance on a prescription)


net_cost
numeric
Cost of the presentation according to the Drug Tariff


actual_cost
numeric
Actual cost including adjustments for bulk purchases, out of pocket expenses, etc. (This is the ‘cost’ field typically used in queries)


quantity
numeric
Number of pills/grams/millilitres/dressings/ampoules prescribed


month
POSIXct
Prescribing month



Rows 10 Columns 12










2.1.2 regional_teams
Open and close dates for regional teams.








Columns






code
character
Regional team code


name
character
Regional team name


open_date
Date
Open date


close_date
Date
Closing date, NA if still open



Rows 10 Columns 4










2.1.3 practices
Includes all the practices in England, updated monthly. Contains latest month’s data only (but includes closed practices). Practices with a setting of 4 are standard GP practices. Other settings are:

0 = Other
1 = WIC Practice
2 = OOH Practice
3 = WIC + OOH Practice
4 = GP Practice
8 = Public Health Service
9 = Community Health Service
10 = Hospital Service
11 = Optometry Service
12 = Urgent & Emergency Care
13 = Hospice
14 = Care Home / Nursing Home
15 = Border Force
16 = Young Offender Institution
17 = Secure Training Centre
18 = Secure Children’s Home
19 = Immigration Removal Centre
20 = Court
21 = Police Custody
22 = Sexual Assault Referral Centre (SARC)
24 = Other









Columns






code
character
Practice code (e.g., E82084)


name
character
Practice name (e.g., HATFIELD ROAD SURGERY)


address1
character
Address line 1 (e.g., 13-15 RUSSELL AVENUE)


address2
character
Address line 2 (e.g., LONDON COLNEY)


address3
character
Address line 3 (e.g., LONDON COLNEY)


address4
character
Address line 4 (town/city) (e.g., HATFIELD)


address5
character
Address line 5


postcode
character
Practice postcode (e.g., AL1 3HD)


location
character
Location of practice in WGS84 format (e.g., 0101000020E6100000F24637A67312CDBF833AED8EDDE14940)


ccg_id
character
Clinical Commissioning Group identifier (e.g., 06N)


pcn_id
character
Primary Care Network identifier (e.g., U73966)


setting
integer
Practice setting code (4 = standard GP practice)


close_date
character
Date practice closed


join_provider_date
character
Date practice joined CCG (e.g., 2013-04-01)


leave_provider_date
character
Date practice left CCG (or closed)


open_date
character
Date practice opened (e.g., 1974-04-01)


status_code
character
Practice status code (A=Active, B=Retired, C=Closed, D=Dormant, P=Proposed, U=Unknown) (e.g., A)



Rows 100 Columns 17










2.1.4 practice_statistics
Total list size and list sizes stratified by gender and age group for each practice, updated monthly. Contains latest month’s data only. Also contains calculated population weightings related to prescribing (STAR-PU and ASTRO-PU).








Columns

POSIXct
The month for which practice statistics are recorded

Count of female patients aged 45-54 years

STAR-PU? as JSON?

character
Practice code (e.g., E86005)



Rows 10 Columns 25

title: 3  Reusable functions
text:
3.1 Connect to BigQuery
All functions here are based on the DBI, bigrquery, dplyr and dbplyr packages to connect to BigQuery and query the OpenPrescribing data in R.
We could write a simple function to connect to BigQuery that we can reuse in our R scripts. This function assumes that you have set the OP_CREDENTIALS environment variable in Section 1.3 and we can define different datasets to connect to by changing the dataset argument (default is hscic).
connect_openprescribing <- function(dataset = "hscic") {
  # Get BigQuery credentials path from environment variable
  credentials_path <- Sys.getenv("OP_CREDENTIALS")

  # Authorise BigQuery
  bigrquery::bq_auth(path = credentials_path)

  # Connect to BigQuery
  DBI::dbConnect(
    bigrquery::bigquery(),
    project = "ebmdatalab",
    dataset = dataset,
    billing = "ebmdatalab"
  )
}
A slighly more robust and flexibly function could also have an option to work with different environment variables and return helpful error messages, see the example below.
Show more flexible and robust function to connect to BigQuery
#' Connect to BigQuery
connect_bq <- function(
  project = "ebmdatalab",
  dataset = "hscic",
  billing = "ebmdatalab",
  credentials_var = "OP_CREDENTIALS"
) {
  # Get the credentials path from environment variable
  credentials_path <- Sys.getenv(credentials_var)

  # Check if environment variable is set
  if (credentials_path == "") {
    cli::cli_abort(c(
      paste("Environment variable", credentials_var, "is not set."),
      "i" = "Please set it in your .Renviron file to point to your BigQuery credentials JSON file.",
      "i" = "You can edit your .Renviron file using: usethis::edit_r_environ()",
      "i" = "See chapters/connect-bq.qmd for detailed setup instructions."
    ))
  }

  # Check if the credentials file exists
  if (!file.exists(credentials_path)) {
    cli::cli_abort(c(
      paste("Credentials file not found at:", credentials_path),
      "i" = paste(
        "The",
        credentials_var,
        "environment variable is set, but the file does not exist."
      ),
      "i" = "Please check the path in your .Renviron file."
    ))
  }

  # Authenticate with BigQuery
  cli::cli_alert_info("Authenticating with BigQuery ...")
  bigrquery::bq_auth(path = credentials_path)

  # Connect to BigQuery
  cli::cli_alert_info(paste0(
    "Connecting to project ",
    project,
    ", dataset ",
    dataset
  ))

  con <- DBI::dbConnect(
    bigrquery::bigquery(),
    project = project,
    dataset = dataset,
    billing = billing
  )

  cli::cli_alert_success("Successfully connected to BigQuery!")
  invisible(con)
}
However, the simple example would do the job and we can establish a connection like this:
con <- connect_openprescribing()
